coredata.dc:title: N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 hybrid as a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-signal readout probe for sensitive and selective detection of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Bezuneh, Terefe Tafese
Bushira, Fuad Abduro
Ofgea, Natinael Mekonnen
Zhang, Chenyu
Li, Haijuan
Jin, Yongdong
coredata.dc:description: A nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N/S-GQDs) and permanganate was employed to prepare a N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 dual-signal readout colorimetric and fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of ascorbic acid (AA). In the detection system, the addition of KMnO4 quenched significantly the fluorescence (FL) of N/S-GQDs, meanwhile, the transparent aqueous dispersion of N/S-GQDs changed into yellow–brown with new absorption peak (maximum absorption at 338 nm). Interestingly, introduction of AA into the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system could recover the quenched fluorescence and result in a decrease in the absorbance (at 338 nm) of the test solution in a concentration dependent manner, and hence demonstrated the potential practical use of the sensing system for AA detection. Based on various spectroscopic studies, it has been concluded that the static as well as inner filter effect was the main quenching mechanisms in the detection system. The dual-response sensor showed linear response in the concentration range between 0.01 and 30 µM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 8.3 nM and 100 nM, respectively, for the fluorescent and colorimetric detections. In addition, the proposed detection system has been successfully applied to the detection of AA in vitamin C tablets. Moreover, the proposed detection strategy is convenient and fast, prevents complex and tedious probe preparation procedure, and capable of visual detection of AA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-signal readout
Ascorbic acid
Colorimetric
Fluorescence
N/S-GQDs
KMnO4

National Key Research and Development Program of China Stem Cell and Translational Research
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Development Program of Jilin Province (Grant 20200201024JC ), and the China Scholarship Council (CSC) (No. 2019DFH008304 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Typical TEM image and B) FT-IR spectrum of the S/N-GQDs C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of S/N-GQDs (Inset: the photograph of the aqueous dispersion of S/N-GQDs under a) day light and b) UV-light at 365 nm) and D) Excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the S/N-GQDs.

Fluorescence (A) and UV–Visible absorption (B) responses of a) N/S-GQDs, b) N/S-GQDs with KMnO4, and c) N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system with AA. Inset: corresponding photos of the related samples. Concentrations of N/S-GQDs, KMnO4, and AA were 60 µg/mL, 100 µM, and 100 µM, respectively.

A) UV–Visible absorption spectra of a) N/S-GQDs, b) N/S-GQDs/KMnO4, c) N/S-GQDs/KMnO4-AA and d) KMnO4; B) High resolution XPS spectrum of Mn 2p of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system; C) FL responses of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system in the presence and absence of AA; and D) Absorption spectrum of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system and excitation spectrum of N/S-GQDs. Concentrations (In A, C, and D) of N/S-GQDs, KMnO4, and AA were 60 µg/mL, 100 µM, and 100 µM, respectively.

Effects of A) concentration of KMnO4 on FL intensity of N/S-GQDs; B) Incubation time effect on UV–Vis absorption of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system with AA; C) pH effect on FL intensity of N/S-GQDs with the addition of KMnO4; D) Ionic concentration effect of NaCl on the FL intensity of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system. The concentrations of N/S-GQDs, KMnO4 and AA were 60 µg/mL, 75 µM, and 100 µM, respectively.

Analytical performances of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 probe with the addition of various concentrations of AA. (A&B) FL responses of the system with AA and corresponding linear range, and (C&D) UV–visible absorption responses of the system with AA and corresponding linear range.
Fluorescence responses of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system towards 100 µM of various potential interfering substances and AA.
Schematic presentations showing A) synthesis procedure for the N/S-GQDs and B) sensing mechanism for AA detection.

head.ce:title: N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 hybrid as a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-signal readout probe for sensitive and selective detection of ascorbic acid

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N/S-GQDs) and permanganate was employed to prepare a N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 dual-signal readout colorimetric and fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of ascorbic acid (AA). In the detection system, the addition of KMnO4 quenched significantly the fluorescence (FL) of N/S-GQDs, meanwhile, the transparent aqueous dispersion of N/S-GQDs changed into yellow–brown with new absorption peak (maximum absorption at 338 nm). Interestingly, introduction of AA into the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system could recover the quenched fluorescence and result in a decrease in the absorbance (at 338 nm) of the test solution in a concentration dependent manner, and hence demonstrated the potential practical use of the sensing system for AA detection. Based on various spectroscopic studies, it has been concluded that the static as well as inner filter effect was the main quenching mechanisms in the detection system. The dual-response sensor showed linear response in the concentration range between 0.01 and 30 µM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 8.3 nM and 100 nM, respectively, for the fluorescent and colorimetric detections. In addition, the proposed detection system has been successfully applied to the detection of AA in vitamin C tablets. Moreover, the proposed detection strategy is convenient and fast, prevents complex and tedious probe preparation procedure, and capable of visual detection of AA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is an extremely important biomolecule in biological system and participates in various biochemical reactions [1,2]. Its main biological function in animal metabolism is related to its antioxidant properties. AA plays a key role in protecting animal cell from various chronic diseases related to oxidative stresses [3–5]. As a result, the detection of AA has attracted considerable research interest. So far, the analytical techniques developed for the detection of AA mainly include surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [6,7], fluorescence [8,9], chemiluminescence [10], colorimetry [11,12], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [13], and liquid chromatography [14–16]. Among these methods, colorimetric and fluorescence sensors have attracted significant research interest due to their easy operation procedure, simplicity, fast-response, and relatively low cost.

Recently, there is a growing research interest in the fabrication of dual-modal sensing probes with capability of generating two signal outputs as the integration of two sensing signals, in a dual-unit or single-unit detection probe, can boost application flexibility, enlarge linear detection range, and improve analytical efficiency and accuracy [17]. In the dual-unit detection probes, two different components are employed to generate two detection signals; while in a single-unit detection probe, the probe contains a single component that is capable of generating two different signals simultaneously [18]. In this regard, the colorimetric and fluorescence signals as output signals are widely employed for the development of dual-modal sensors. And plenty of work has been reported for the detection of various target analytes including cardiac troponin I (cTnI) [19,20], alpha-fetoprotein (AFP)[21], anions [22,23], metal ions [24–27], H2O2 [28], and cysteine [29,30].

Despite the significant advantages of dual-signal colorimetric and fluorescence sensors, there are still very limited reports on dual-response colorimetric and fluorescence sensors for the detection of AA. To this end, Li et al. proposed a fluorescence and colorimetric dual-responsive AA sensor by employing cobalt-doped carbon quantum dots (Co-CQDs) [31]. In the detection system, the fluorescent detection of AA was realized based on the recovery of the quenched fluorescence of Co-CQDs/Fe3+ system; whereas the oxidase-mimetic property of Co-CQDs assists the catalytic formation of blue oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (ox-TMB, the colour change from blue to transparent) which enables the colorimetric detection of AA. In another study, Chen’s group reported the use of CDs as fluorescent and colorimetric dual responsive probe for the detection of AA [32]. Wu et al. also realized sensitive detection of AA based on fluorescence recovery and color change of the 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine/gold nanocluster system [33].

In this study, we employed N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system as a new dual-signal responsive probe for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of AA (Scheme 1 ). As far as we are concerned, this is the first to employ QDs/KMnO4 hybrid as dual responsive probe for the detection of AA. In the detection system, the addition of KMnO4 can not only effectively quench the fluorescence of N/S-GQDs, but also transform the color of the test solution from transparent to brown color with the appearance of a distinct new absorption peak (at ∼338 nm). Interestingly, the addition of AA into the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system can recover the quenched fluorescence and also decrease the absorption of the test solution at 338 nm in a concentration dependent manner to realize sensitive detection of AA. The as-proposed sensor showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of AA. Moreover, the applicability of the as-prepared probe for the detection of AA in real vitamin C tablets was examined.

Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), glucose, sucrose, and L-ascorbic acid were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Factory (Shanghai, China). Thiourea was obtained from Guangdong Guanghua Sci. Tech. Co. Ltd. (China). Citric acid was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Dialysis bags were provided by Yuan Ye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Solutions of metal ions were prepared from respective nitrate, chloride, and sulfate salts. Double distilled water was used to prepare all aqueous solutions. All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Malaysia) was used to obtain fluorescence spectra. UV–Visible absorption spectra of N/S-GQDs were collected using an ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (Analytik Jena, Germany). The size and shape of the N/S-GQDs were obtained from high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (Philips Tecnai F20). A Bruker infrared Vertex 70 interferometer was employed to perform Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic analysis. Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer was employed to perform X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) investigation.

The highly fluorescent blue emitting N/S-GQDs probe was synthesized based on our method previously reported [34]. Briefly, a mixture of citric acid and thiourea in a molar ratio of 1:2 was heated for 30 min at 180 °C in a preheated silicon oil bath to obtain dark-brown product (N/S-GQDs). Finally, the obtained product was dispersed in water and centrifuged for 30 min at 5,000 rpm to obtain tawny dispersion. Then, the aqueous dispersion of N/S-GQDs was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with molecular weight cut-of 1 kDa against double distilled water for 24 h. Finally, the dialyzed product was lyophilized to obtain solid product and stored in dark container in the refrigerator at 4 °C for the next experiments.

The applicability of the as-proposed sensor in real sample analysis was evaluated using Vitamin C tablets. First, five pieces of vitamin C tablets purchased from local pharmacy were ground into fine powder by using a mortar. Then, 120 mg of the Vitamin C powder was dissolved in double distilled water. The as-prepared solution was subjected to centrifugation for 20 min at 12,000 rpm to remove any insoluble components. The resulting supernatant was then stored in the dark at 4 °C until required for the next experiments.

For the detection of AA, a 60 µg/mL aqueous dispersion of N/S-GQDs was transferred into 2 mL eppendorf tube. Then, aqueous solution of KMnO4 with final concentration of 75 µM was transferred into the above mixture and incubated for about 10 min in a dark place at room temperature (RT). Next, an appropriate amount of AA was added into the mixture and the final volume was adjusted by adding ultra-pure water. Finally, the mixture was incubated at RT for 10 min and fluorescence responses were recorded at an excitation and emission wavelength of 370 and 455 nm, respectively. The same procedure was followed for colorimetric detection of AA and UV–Vis absorption spectrum was recorded. All measurements were repeated 3 times. For real sample analysis, the standard solution of AA was replaced by real sample solutions prepared in section 2.4.

The N/S-GQDs was synthesized by solid phase pyrolysis of citric acid (carbon source) and thiourea (nitrogen and sulfur source). As presented in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1 A), the as-synthesized N/S-GQDs showed well distributed spherical morphology with the size distribution in the range of 1.8–4.5 nm and an average size of 2.8 nm (Fig. S1). Also, the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1B) of N/S-GQDs revealed the presence of various surface functional groups, such as hydroxyl (O-H)/amines (N-H) (stretching vibration around 3670 to 2700 cm−1), carboxylic (CO) (stretching vibrations at 1700 cm−1), and amide (stretching vibration at 1600 cm−1). More importantly, the stretching vibrations at 1400 cm−1 (C–N) and 1200 cm−1 (C–S) proved effective doping of N and S atoms into GQDs. Moreover, the elemental maps by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was performed to further confirm the effective doping of S and N in the N/S-GQDs. As presented in Fig. S2, the EDX result proved the existence of N and S atoms in the quantum dot. The presence of these surface functional groups endows high aqueous dispersibility, stability and optical properties of the N/S-GQDs.

Furthermore, XPS spectrum of N/S-GQDs was studied and the result was presented in Fig. S3. As presented in the Fig. S3A, XPS survey spectra showed the presence of O (531.08 eV), N (398.08 eV), C (284.08 eV), and S (162.08 eV). In addition the high resolution spectrum (Fig. S3 B-E) showed the presence of various functionalities. For instance C1s (Fig. S3B) showed the presence of sp2 C (283.68 eV), sp3 C from C–N/C–S and C–O (285.25 eV), and CO (288.4 eV) [35]. As presented in Fig. S3C, the two peaks of O1s at 532.5 and 531.1 eV showed the presence of CO and C-O-C/COH and CO groups [36]. The S2p (Fig. S3D) showed the presence of C-S-C bond (162.38 eV) and SO (166.78 eV)) [37]. Moreover, as presented in Fig. S3E, the high resolution N1s spectra showed the presence of pyridinic N atoms (398.50 eV) [38]. In general, XPS, FT-IR, and EDX results are in agreement with each other and proved the presence of various surface functional groups including, carboxylic, amines and more importantly the effective doping of S and N atoms into GQDs.

The optical properties of S/N-GQDs were examined using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of S/N-GQDs (Fig. 1C) revealed the presence of various electronic transitions at 260 nm (π-π*, sp2 carbon) and 340 nm (n-π*, C = O). According to literatures, the luminescence properties of CDs could be related to the presence of π-π* electronic transitions in their structure [39–41]. The luminescence properties of the tawny aqueous dispersion (Fig. 1C, Inset-a) of N/S-GQDs were proved by emission of bright blue fluorescence under UV-light at 365 nm (Fig. 1C, Inset-b). Furthermore, as presented in Fig. 1D, the N/S-GQDs showed excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission with maximum emission intensity (at 455 nm) at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The fluorescence emission wavelength showed a red shift with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. S4). This could be attributed to the presence of non-uniformity in size in the quantum dots [42,43].

As the stability of probe plays a key role in determining its analytical performance, we tested the stability of N/S-GQDs at various conditions. The aqueous dispersibility (stability) of N/S-GQDs was tested using Zeta-potential measurement. As presented in Fig. S5, the N/S-GQDs displayed a negative Zeta-potential value (−19 mV) in aqueous solution. In addition, the N/S-GQDs showed pH dependent fluorescence emission intensity with maximum response at pH 6 and 7 (Fig. S6A). Moreover, the aqueous solution of N/S-GQDs stored for 2 months at 4 °C and RT retained 70 and 50 % of its initial fluorescence intensity (Fig. S6B), respectively, and showed no sign of precipitation. Next, the applicability of the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system as a fluorescence and colorimetric probe for the detection of AA was examined. First, the fluorescence response of N/S-GQDs in the presence and absence of KMnO4 and AA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of N/S-GQDs (curve a) was effectively quenched after the addition of KMnO4 (curve b). In comparison to various potential fluorescence quenching metal ions tested, KMnO4 showed excellent fluorescence quenching ability (Fig. S7). Hence, KMnO4 can be used as an effective fluorescence quencher of N/S-GQDs to construct signal “off” type fluorescence sensors. Interestingly, the quenched fluorescence could be well recovered (signal-on) by the addition of AA (Fig. 2A, curve c). The quenching and FL recovery phenomenon can be clearly seen by the naked eye under UV-light at 365 nm (Fig. 2A, Inset).

Next, the potential applicability of the N/S-GQDs to construct a colorimetric sensor was evaluated. Fig. 2B shows the UV–Vis absorption response of N/S-GQDs in the presence and absence of KMnO4 and AA. The result demonstrated that, in the presence of KMnO4, the absorption profile of N/S-GQDs (curve a) significantly changed with the formation of a slightly blue-shifted peak at 338 nm with enhanced absorbance (curve b). Moreover, the transparent color of the aqueous dispersion of N/S-GQDs (Inset a) also changed to brown color (Inset b) which is obviously different from purple color of the added KMnO4. However, in the presence of AA, the brown color faded back to transparent (Inset c). In addition, the new absorbance profile disappeared and an absorption profile similar to that of N/S-GQDs was obtained (curve c). Hence, the change in color and absorption intensity at 338 nm can be used to quantitatively determine the concentration of target AA.

The sensing mechanism of the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 as a dual-signal readout fluorescence and colorimetric sensor for AA were studied using various analytical techniques. First, the interaction of N/S-GQDs with KMnO4 was studied. Fig. 3 A shows the UV–Visible absorption spectra of N/S-GQDs with and without the addition of KMnO4. As presented in the curve d, KMnO4 exhibited strong absorption bands in the UV and visible region with typical absorption peaks at 210, 310, 350, 505, 525 and 545 nm. The as-prepared N/S-GQDs showed absorption peaks at 260, 340, and a weak shoulder peak in the range of 400–500 nm (curve a). Interestingly, upon mixing N/S-GQDs with KMnO4, the absorption profile of the resulting solution changed with the appearance of new and intense absorption peak centered at 338 nm (curve b). In particular, all the characteristic peaks of KMnO4 disappeared, indicating its reduction into lower oxidation state as it has also been observed in previous reports [44]. In addition, as presented in Fig. 3A, the transparent aqueous dispersion of N/S-GQDs (Inset a) transformed into yellow–brown color (Inset b) in the presence of KMnO4 (purple color, Inset d). This further proved the reduction of permanganate into lower oxidation states, as previously reported [45–47]. Moreover, with increasing concentration of KMnO4, the transparent appearance of the N/S-GQDs became darker and darker with significant increase in absorption intensity at 338 nm (Fig. S8, A&B). Based on these results, it can be concluded that N/S-GQDs can in situ reduce permanganate into lower oxidation states as there were no other reducing agents in the test solution.

To get further insights into the redox reaction between N/S-GQDs and KMnO4, we conducted further UV–Vis and XPS characterizations. Note that, the absorption intensity of the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4-AA system (Fig. 3A, curve c) was significantly lower than that of N/S-GQDs alone (cf. curve a). To get clear insight, we added various concentration of KMnO4 into N/S-GQDs and after incubating for some time, we added excess AA into these mixtures. Interestingly, the absorption profile of N/S-GQDs obviously gradually decreased with increasing the concentration of KMnO4 (Fig. S9). It indicated the presence of possible sacrificial redox reaction of N/S-GQDs with KMnO4. In agreement to our result, Unnikrishnan et al. observed similar sacrificial redox reaction of CDs with KMnO4 which resulted in the formation of various manganese oxide species [48]. Moreover, the XPS data confirmed the occurrence of permanganate reduction into various lower oxidations states (Fig. S10). As presented in Fig. S3A, the XPS survey spectrum of the N/S-GQDs showed the presence of only O 1s (531.08 eV), N 1s (398.08 eV), C 1s (284.08 eV), and S 2p (162.08 eV) peaks. However, in the XPS survey spectrum of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system, an additional peak of Mn 2p at 641.3 eV was observed (Fig. S10A). The high resolution XPS spectrum of Mn 2p (Fig. 4 B) showed two main peaks at 641.3 eV (Mn 2p3/2) and 653 eV (Mn 2p1/2) with a spin-energy separation of 11.8 eV. The result further proved the reduction of permanganate into lower oxidation states which is in accordance with previously reported results [49–51].

Next, the applicability of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system as a fluorescent and colorimetric probe for the detection of AA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3C, the original fluorescence emission intensity of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system was in a quenched state due to ground state interaction of N/S-GQDs with KMnO4 resulting in static quenching [52,53]. Moreover, the inner filter effect could be there as the newly formed absorption profile completely overlapped with the excitation spectrum of N/S-GQDs (cf. Fig. 3D). Interestingly, owing to excellent redox reaction between the AA and permanganates [54], the quenched fluorescence could be recovered, with a ∼5.5-fold enhancement. This was proved by XPS results as the Mn 2p peak in the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system (Fig. S10A) disappeared after treated with AA (Fig. S10B). Moreover, the brown color of the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 test solution (see Fig. 3A, inset b) decolorized (Fig. 3A, inset c) and the absorption peak at 338 nm significantly decreased (Fig. 3A, curve c). The extent of fluorescence recovery and decrease in absorption profile could be quantitatively related to the concentration of target AA.

To obtain optimum performance of the detection system the effects of various experimental variables such as concentration of KMnO4, time of incubation, pH, and ionic concentration have been optimized. First, the effects of the concentration of KMnO4 (25, 50, 75, and 100 µM) on fluorescence and absorption of N/S-GQDs were studied. As presented in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of N/S-GQDs significantly and gradually decreased with increasing the concentration of KMnO4. Although higher concentration of KMnO4 resulted in higher fluorescence quenching (∼100 %), preliminary study showed that higher concentration of KMnO4 resulted in poor sensitivity. As a result, 75 µM of KMnO4 was selected as an optimum concentration both in fluorescence and colorimetric sensing experiments.

Fig. 4B shows the effect of incubation time of the added AA on absorption of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system. The result showed that absorption at 338 nm gradually decreased with increasing incubation time until 10 min, beyond in which there was no significant change in absorption profile. Hence, 10 min was taken as an optimum incubation time for the next experiments. Next, the influence of pH (from 5 to 10) on the fluorescence response of N/S-GQDs without (F0) and with the addition of KMnO4 (F) was investigated. Fig. 4C presents the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F0-F)/F0 against various pHs. The result demonstrated that the highest fluorescence quenching was obtained at a pH of 7. Hence, to simplify the experimental operation, ultrapure water was directly used to prepare test solutions. Moreover, the effect of ionic concentration of NaCl on the fluorescence response of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system was investigated (Fig. 4D). The result presented good stability of the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 system against various ionic concentrations (even at high ionic conditions of 1 mM).

At an optimized condition, the analytical performance of the N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 probe as a fluorescence and colorimetric sensor for the detection of AA was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5 A&B, the FL intensity gradually and significantly increased with increasing concentrations of AA in the detection system. The FL responses showed a good linearity in the concentration range from 0.01 to 25 µM (Fig. 5B) and the LOD computed based on 3SD (blank/slope, n = 11) [55] was 8.3 nM. Moreover, the colorimetric response of the as-constructed probe was tested by measuring UV–Vis absorbance responses (Fig. 5C&D). The result demonstrated that absorption profile of the probe at 338 nm significantly and gradually decreased with increasing the AA concentration (Fig. 5C). As shown in Fig. 5D, the colorimetric probe showed a good linearity in the concentration range from 0.1 to 30 µM and a LOD of 100 nM. Moreover, the probe is capable of visual detection of AA as the quenched fluorescence recovery (Fig. 5A, inset) and decolorization of yellow–brown color (Fig. 5B, inset) of the test solution can be seen by nacked eye under 365 nm UV-light and day light, respectively.

Compared to previously reported dual-mode detection of AA, our method showed superiority in its sensitivity. For instance, Wu’s group employed Cobalt-doped CQDs with peroxidase-mimetic activity for AA detection through both fluorometric and colorimetric methods [31]. The detection system presented a lower sensitivity (LOD) of 18 µM for fluorescence and 0.27 μM for colorimetric detections which is lower compared to our method which is 8.3 nM and 100 nM, respectively. Table S1 presents the comparison of analytical performance of the proposed dual response sensor with previously published results. The as-constructed dual response sensor showed lower or comparable sensitivity, easy-probe preparation, fast-response, and capable of visual detection for the detection of AA.

The feasibility of the as-constructed sensor for real sample application was evaluated by detecting AA in vitamin C tablets. In a typical procedure, various solutions prepared from vitamin C tablet were spiked with known concentration of standard AA. Then fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected to evaluate the percent recovery. Table S2 presents the result of the recovery experiments using the proposed fluorescence and colorimetric method. The as-proposed fluorescence and colorimetric sensor showed recoveries within the range of 86 to 103 %. Furthermore, the relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) were calculated to be 0.6–2.3 %. The lower %RSD and satisfactory recoveries proved the reliability and accuracy of the sensor for the detection of AA in real samples.

The selectivity of the proposed sensing method for AA detection was further evaluated. The effect of various possible interfering species including tannic acid, lactose, L-Cystein, sucrose, glucose, thiourea, citric acid, and glycine on the absorption and fluorescence of N/S-GQDs-KMnO4 system were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S11A, the absorption profile only changed in the presence of AA. Also, none of the tested interfering substances recover obviously the quenched fluorescence of N/S-GQDs/KMnO4, except for AA (Fig. 6 & S11B). The results demonstrated good selectivity of the method for the detection of AA. Moreover, as presented in Fig. S12, the fluorescence intensity of N/S-GQDs can be switched “off” by adding KMnO4 and then could be turned “on” again by adding AA. In repeated procedure the fluorescence intensity of the system can be switched off–on in sequential addition of KMnO4 and AA. This demonstrated the reusability of the sensor in consecutive detection of AA.

In this study, we realized sensitive and selective detection of AA by employing N/S-GQDs/KMnO4 based fluorescence and colorimetric dual responsive AA nanoprobe. In the detection system, KMnO4 could not only effectively quench the fluorescence of N/S-GQDs but also transform the transparent color of N/S-GQDs into yellow-brownish appearance; the addition of target AA into the detection system could effectively recover the quenched fluorescence and also decolorize the test solution into transparent with decrease in absorbance of the test solution. The extent of fluorescence recovery and decrease in absorption intensity was exploited for the detection of AA with excellent sensitivity and selectivity. In addition, the detection system avoids complex and tedious probe preparation procedure, and capable of visual detection of AA. The potential applicability of the proposed sensor for sensitive detection of AA in real samples was evaluated using vitamin C tablets, which showed promising potential for its application in complex matrices.

Terefe Tafese Bezuneh: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Fuad Abduro Bushira: Writing – review & editing. Natinael Mekonnen Ofgea: Writing – review & editing. Chenyu Zhang: Investigation. Haijuan Li: . Yongdong Jin: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Development Program of Jilin Province (Grant 20200201024JC), and the China Scholarship Council (CSC) (No. 2019DFH008304).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109837.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
